meta.description: Shadowkin origin story - born between dimensions, hunted and feared

shd_001.title: Between
text: You remember the moment you stopped being real.

You were seven. Sitting on the floor of a transit hub, watching the legs of strangers pass through pools of fluorescent light. Your mother's hand was warm around yours. She was talking to someone, her voice a low murmur above your head, and you were tracing patterns in the grime between the floor tiles with your free finger.
Then the world folded.

It didn't break. It didn't shatter. It folded, like someone creasing a piece of paper with you caught in the middle. One second you were solid, a child with weight and warmth and a mother's hand anchoring you to the ground. The next second you were smoke. Your fingers passed through the floor. Your mother's grip closed on empty air. You heard her scream your name, but it came from very far away, muffled by a membrane of reality that had suddenly decided you didn't belong on either side of it.
That was thirteen years ago. You've been flickering ever since.

choices.text: Remember what happened next
Push the memory away

shd_002.title: The First Phase
text: You fell through the floor. Through solid duracrete and rebar and the guts of the building, through pipes and wiring and the crawlspace where rats nested in insulation foam. You fell and you didn't stop because nothing could stop you. You were less than air. Less than light.

You landed in darkness. Not the darkness of an unlit room but the darkness between. The space that exists in the gap when reality blinks. It was cold there in a way that had nothing to do with temperature. It was the cold of absence, of a place where heat had never existed because the concept hadn't been invented yet.

You screamed but nothing came out. You had no lungs. You had no throat. You were a thought shaped like a child, suspended in nothing, and the nothing was looking back at you with an attention that felt like hunger.

Then you snapped back. Gasping on the sub-level floor, solid again, your mother's screams echoing down the maintenance shaft you'd fallen through. Two floors. You'd phased through two floors of solid structure and landed on concrete without a scratch.

choices.text: Remember your mother's face when she found you
Remember the scientists who came after

shd_003.title: The Present
text: You shove the memory down into the dark place where you keep all the things that hurt too much to hold. It's crowded in there. The dark place is filling up, and sometimes the memories leak through, the same way you leak through walls when you lose concentration.

Focus on now. You're in the tunnels beneath Sector 7, the old maintenance network that runs under the Lower District like a second city built from rust and dripping pipes. This is where you live. Not by choice, but because down here the walls are thick enough that when you phase in your sleep, you don't fall through to somewhere worse.

The concrete around you hums with the vibration of the city above. Millions of people going about their lives, solid and certain, never wondering what it feels like to reach for a door handle and watch your fingers pass through it like it isn't there. Like you aren't there.

Your bedroll is wedged between two service conduits. A battery-powered lamp casts a circle of yellow light that makes the shadows dance. You've learned to hate dancing shadows. You've learned to hate a lot of things about shadows.

choices.text: Check your supplies
Someone's coming down the tunnel

shd_004.title: Mother
text: She found you twenty minutes later, after maintenance workers cut open the access panel to the sub-level. She came down the ladder so fast she slipped and twisted her ankle, and she still crawled the last ten meters to reach you.

She held you so tight you could feel her heartbeat through her coat. She was shaking. You were shaking. But even as she whispered that everything was fine, everything was okay, you could see it in her eyes. The thing that lived there from that day forward.
Fear.

Not fear of what had happened. Fear of you. Of what you were becoming. She loved you, God, she loved you with every cell in her body, but she was afraid of her own child, and you were seven years old and you could see it, and it was the first time you understood that love and fear could live in the same heart without killing each other.
She took you home. She locked the doors. She told you never to tell anyone what happened. And for two years, that worked.

choices.text: What happened after two years
Return to the present

shd_005.title: The Scientists
text: Meridian Applied Sciences. Division 9. The department that officially doesn't exist, funded by a budget that officially doesn't get allocated, staffed by people whose employment records are officially blank.

They came three weeks after your first phase. Two men and a woman in grey coats, carrying equipment that hummed at a frequency that made your teeth ache. They spoke to your mother in the hallway while you pressed your ear against your bedroom door and listened to words you didn't understand. Dimensional resonance. Phase variance. Subject viability.
Your mother said no. She said it quietly, firmly, with the steel that lived underneath her fear. No, you can't take my child. No, you can't run your tests. No, I won't sign your forms.

They left. But they came back. They always came back. Different faces, same grey coats, same humming equipment. And each time your mother said no, the requests became less like requests and more like warnings.

choices.text: Remember when they stopped asking
Return to the present

shd_006.title: Supplies
text: You check the duffel bag that serves as your pantry, wardrobe, and medicine cabinet. The inventory is grim. Two nutrient bars, the cheap kind that taste like compressed cardboard and despair. A water bottle, half full, refilled from the condensation traps you've set along the pipe junctions. A change of clothes so worn they're more patch than fabric.

At the bottom of the bag, wrapped in an oily cloth, is the one thing you'd kill to keep: a phase stabilizer. Homemade. Cobbled together from stolen medical components and scrap electronics by a back-alley tech who owed you a favor. It looks like a collar made of wire and blinking diodes, ugly as sin, but when you wear it, the involuntary phasing stops. Mostly.
The battery indicator blinks amber. Maybe twelve hours of charge left. After that, you're back to raw willpower, which has a failure rate you'd rather not think about.
Your stomach growls. The nutrient bars stare back at you with all the appeal of a death sentence.

choices.text: Eat a nutrient bar and conserve energy
Save the food and head topside to find more

shd_007.title: Footsteps
text: The sound comes from the east tunnel. Footsteps, plural, moving fast. Not the cautious shuffle of tunnel dwellers who know the terrain. Not the measured stride of maintenance workers. This is the sound of people who don't care about being heard, which means they're either stupid or confident, and in the tunnels, confident is worse.

Your body reacts before your mind catches up. The familiar tingling starts at your fingertips and crawls up your arms. Your outline blurs. The edges of you become suggestions rather than statements. It's the phase response, your body's panic button, the thing it does when fight-or-flight kicks in and your particular biology adds a third option: become intangible.

You clamp down on it. Hard. Phasing in the tunnels is dangerous. If you lose cohesion near the water mains, you could materialize inside a pipe. If you drift too deep, you might hit the old reactor shielding, and whatever's on the other side of that, nobody comes back from.
The footsteps are getting closer. You can hear voices now. Low, urgent, speaking in the clipped shorthand of people on a job.

choices.text: Hide in the shadows and watch
Phase through the wall to escape
Stand your ground

shd_008.title: The Night They Came
text: You were nine. It was the middle of the night, and you were dreaming about falling through floors again, the recurring nightmare that never quite stopped feeling real. Your mother shook you awake, her face lit by the blue emergency light she kept by her bed.
Get dressed. Now. Don't turn on the lights.

You could hear them outside. Not knocking. Not this time. The hiss of a plasma cutter working through the door's security bolts. The low chatter of tactical comms. The hum of that equipment, that frequency that made your bones vibrate like tuning forks.

Your mother had a bag packed. She always had a bag packed. She grabbed your hand and pulled you to the back window, the one that opened onto the fire escape. She was calm. Terrifyingly calm. The kind of calm that comes from having rehearsed this moment in your head every night for two years.
She got you out. Down the fire escape, through the alley, into the crowd of night-shift workers heading for the transit hub. She held your hand so tight her knuckles were white.
You never went home again.

choices.text: Remember the years of running
Remember when you lost her

shd_009.title: Sustenance
text: You unwrap the nutrient bar and bite into it. The texture is somewhere between stale bread and industrial sealant. The taste is a crime against every culture that ever invented cooking. But it's calories, and calories are the difference between controlled phasing and the involuntary kind that leaves you embedded in infrastructure.

As you chew, you think about the stabilizer's battery. Twelve hours. You could head to the Scrap Yards and look for a compatible power cell, but the yards are contested territory. The salvage crews guard their claims with welding torches and bad attitudes. Or you could try Mira, the tech who built the stabilizer in the first place, but Mira's shop is in the Market District, and the Market District means crowds, and crowds mean risk.

Every time you brush against another person, there's a chance. A small one, but real. That the contact triggers a micro-phase, your hand passing through their arm, their shoulder, their chest. Most people don't notice. Some do. The ones who notice scream.

choices.text: Head to the Scrap Yards for a power cell
Go see Mira in the Market District

shd_010.title: Going Up
text: You stash the nutrient bars back in the duffel and sling it over your shoulder. The stabilizer goes around your neck, its weight familiar and grounding, a reminder that you're solid, you're here, you're not going to drift through the pavement and into whatever waits below.

The nearest topside exit is a maintenance ladder that opens into an alley behind a noodle stand in Sector 7. The owner, an ancient woman named Bao, knows you use the access hatch. She doesn't ask questions. You leave credits on her counter when you have them, which isn't often enough to ease the guilt.

You climb. The ladder is slick with condensation, and twice your foot slips on the rungs. Each slip sends a spike of adrenaline through your chest, and each spike makes your edges blur. You breathe through it. In four, hold seven, out eight. The pattern Mira taught you, the one that keeps the phase response from activating when your body wants to panic.
The hatch opens with a screech of corroded hinges. Night air hits your face, thick with rain and the chemical tang of lower-level atmospheric processing. You're topside.

choices.text: Head for the noodle stand
Stick to the alleys and move toward the market

shd_011.title: Watching from Dark
text: You kill the lamp and press yourself against the wall. In the darkness, you're almost invisible. Your skin does that thing it does when you're afraid, the pigment shifting, the outline softening, your body instinctively trying to become less real. Not a full phase, just enough to blur into the background like a smudge on a photograph.

Three figures round the corner. Two are carrying a stretcher between them, and on the stretcher is a shape wrapped in thermal blankets. Small. Child-sized. The shape is trembling, and where the blankets have slipped, you can see skin that flickers between solid and translucent.

The third figure walks ahead, sweeping a handheld scanner in slow arcs. The scanner emits a soft blue pulse that makes your stabilizer crackle with interference. You recognize the frequency. You'd recognize it anywhere.
Division 9.

They're still hunting. After all these years, they're still hunting your kind. And they've found another one. A child, phasing and terrified, being carried through the tunnels by people who see them as a specimen.

choices.text: Follow them quietly
You have to help that child
It's not your problem. Survive.

shd_012.title: Through the Wall
text: You reach for the phase. Not the panic-triggered kind, not the involuntary slip that haunts your nightmares, but the deliberate shift. The one you've spent years learning to control. You close your eyes and focus on the boundary between here and there, and you push.

The wall isn't a wall anymore. It's a suggestion. A polite request from physics that you're choosing to decline. You step forward and the concrete parts around you like cold water, the molecules of your body sliding between the molecules of the structure in a dance that shouldn't be possible but is.

For a moment, you're inside the wall. Darkness. Pressure. The alien sensation of existing in the same space as something solid. You can feel the rebar like a whisper of resistance, the electrical conduits like warm threads. Then you're through, stumbling into a parallel maintenance corridor, gasping, solid again.

The phase took seven seconds. Seven seconds of being nothing. It never gets easier. The between-space is always there during a phase, that hungry dark, pressing against the edges of your consciousness like a tongue probing a wound.

choices.text: Keep moving deeper into the tunnels
Circle back and see who those people were

shd_013.title: Standing Ground
text: You plant your feet. You've spent too many years running. Running from Division 9, running from scared neighbors, running from your own reflection. Whatever's coming down that tunnel can meet you standing up.

The first figure rounds the corner and freezes. A woman, mid-thirties, wearing a patched jacket with the sleeves cut off. Her arms are covered in tattoos that seem to move in the dim light, shifting patterns of dark ink that crawl across her skin like living things. Her eyes lock onto you, and for a second neither of you moves.
Then she smiles. It's not a friendly smile. It's the smile of someone who's found something they were looking for.

'You're the one from the east tunnels,' she says. 'The one who walks through walls.' Behind her, two more figures emerge. A massive man with a shaved head and a teenage boy whose outline flickers the same way yours does. He's one of you. A Shadowkin.
The boy stares at you with wide eyes. You stare back. You've never met another one. In thirteen years of phasing, you've never seen anyone else do what you do.

choices.text: "Who are you people?"
Focus on the boy. "You're like me."

shd_014.title: Years of Running
text: Three years. That's how long you and your mother stayed ahead of them. Three years of new districts, new names, new schools where you sat in the back and tried not to touch anyone. Three years of your mother working double shifts under false identities, coming home with shadows under her eyes and the kind of exhaustion that lives in the bones.

You learned to control the phasing during those years. Not perfectly, not even well, but enough. Enough to keep from falling through your bed at night. Enough to catch yourself when a loud noise or a sudden fright sent the world sliding sideways. Your mother timed the episodes. They were getting longer. Lasting from seconds to minutes. And each time, it was harder to come back.

She never said it, but you could see the math behind her eyes. The progression. The trajectory. She was calculating how long before a phase lasted forever, before you slipped through reality and didn't come back. She was calculating how long she had left with her child.
You moved eleven times in three years. The last time was Sector 7.

choices.text: Remember Sector 7
Return to the present

shd_015.title: When You Lost Her
text: Sector 7 was supposed to be the last stop. Deep enough in the Lower District that Division 9's jurisdiction got tangled in the territorial disputes between corporate security franchises. Your mother found a room, found work, found something that almost looked like stability if you squinted hard enough.
You were twelve when they found you again.

It was a Tuesday. You remember because Tuesdays were the day your mother got paid, and she always brought home real food on Tuesdays, not nutrient bars but actual rice and protein that tasted like it came from something that had once been alive. You were sitting on the floor of your room, doing homework on a cracked datapad, when the door exploded inward.

Not Division 9 this time. Worse. Private contractors, the kind corporations hire when they want plausible deniability. No grey coats, no scanner equipment, just body armor and neural stunners and the cold efficiency of people who get paid by the job.

Your mother fought. She'd learned, over the years. She'd prepared. She had a weapon under the mattress, a taser she'd bought from a market stall, and she used it. She dropped one of them, the big one in front, and she screamed at you to run, and you ran, and you phased through the back wall without even trying, your body choosing survival over solidarity.
You never saw her again.

choices.text: You survived. That's what she wanted.
The guilt has never gone away

shd_016.title: The Scrap Yards
text: The Scrap Yards stretch across three city blocks of decommissioned infrastructure, a graveyard of technology stacked in mountains of corroded metal and gutted circuitry. Corporate waste that trickles down to the Lower District like toxic rain. For people like you, it's a hardware store with no prices and no cashiers, just the constant threat of salvage crews who defend their territory with violence and welding torches.

You approach from the south, keeping to the shadows between the stacks. Your stabilizer hums against your collarbone, its amber light blinking steadily. Twelve hours. Maybe eleven now. Every minute costs you charge, and every minute without charge costs you control.

The yard is busy tonight. You can see the blue-white flicker of cutting torches deeper in, hear the clang of metal on metal as crews strip components from decommissioned machinery. The power cells you need would be in the electronics heap, the mountain of discarded devices near the eastern wall. That's deep in claimed territory.
A patrol passes, two scrappers with pipe wrenches and the territorial swagger of people who've been doing this long enough to feel ownership over garbage.

choices.text: Phase through the stacks to reach the electronics heap unseen
Try to negotiate with the scrappers
Wait for the patrol to pass and sneak in

shd_017.title: Market District
text: The Market District pulses with desperate commerce. Vendors hawk everything from bootleg memory stacks to synthetic organ replacements, their stalls lit by strings of mismatched LEDs that paint the street in carnival colors. The crowd is thick, a river of bodies flowing between the stalls, and you navigate it the way you navigate everything: carefully, with your hands in your pockets and your elbows tight against your sides.

Don't touch anyone. Don't brush against anyone. Keep the phase response locked down. You repeat it like a mantra, timing your breathing to the rhythm of your steps. In four, hold seven, out eight. The crowd jostles around you, close and warm and alive, and every accidental contact sends a spark of fear up your spine.

Mira's shop is at the end of the strip, a hole-in-the-wall wedged between a noodle bar and a body-mod parlor. The sign above the door reads MIRA'S MODS AND FIXES in hand-painted letters, half of which have faded to ghosts. The door is reinforced steel, which in this district means she's either paranoid or smart. Probably both.
You knock. Three taps, pause, two taps. The code she gave you.

choices.text: Wait for Mira to answer
Check if anyone followed you

shd_018.title: Bao's Noodles
text: The noodle stand is a counter and four stools bolted to the sidewalk under a sheet of corrugated metal. Bao stands behind the counter, stirring a pot of broth that smells like the best thing that's ever existed. She's eighty if she's a day, four feet eleven, with hands that move through the steam with the precision of a surgeon.

She sees you climb out of the hatch and doesn't blink. Just reaches under the counter and pulls out a bowl, filling it without being asked. The broth is thin but hot, the noodles are real wheat, and there's actual protein floating in it, grey and rubbery but unmistakably meat-adjacent.
'You look like death,' Bao says, pushing the bowl across the counter. 'Eat.'

You don't have credits to pay. She knows this. She feeds you anyway, the same way she feeds the other strays who crawl out of the maintenance tunnels. You've never asked why. Some questions are better left as kindness.
The rain hammers the metal roof above you. For a moment, sitting at Bao's counter with hot food in your hands, the world feels almost bearable.

choices.text: Ask Bao if she's heard any rumors
Eat quickly and move on to the market

shd_019.title: Following Division 9
text: You follow at a distance, your feet silent on the tunnel floor. Years of living underground have taught you how to move without sound, where to place your weight to avoid the grating that squeaks, the puddles that splash, the gravel that crunches. You're a shadow following shadows, and the irony isn't lost on you.

The Division 9 team moves with purpose, navigating the tunnel network with the help of their scanner. They know where they're going. They've been down here before, mapping routes, identifying bolt-holes, tracking the phase signatures that your kind leave behind like fingerprints in the fabric of reality.

The child on the stretcher has stopped trembling. Either they've been sedated or they've exhausted themselves into stillness. You can see the outline of a stabilizer collar around their neck, not a homemade one like yours but a sleek, medical-grade device that probably cost more than you've earned in your entire life.

They're heading toward the old pumping station. You know this section of the tunnels. There's a freight elevator there that connects to the surface, to a loading dock behind a pharmaceutical warehouse. That's their extraction point.

choices.text: Get ahead of them and sabotage the elevator
Phase through the walls to reach the child
Memorize their route and report to others

shd_020.title: The Rescue
text: You don't think. You phase.

The world goes sideways. Concrete becomes mist. You move through the wall like a knife through smoke, angling your trajectory to come out right beside the stretcher. For three seconds you're in the between-space, the hungry dark pressing against you with its cold attention, and then you're through, materializing in a crouch next to the child.

The Division 9 team reacts fast. The scanner operator swings a neural stunner toward you, and the blue arc of electricity crackles through the space where your head was a heartbeat ago. You've already grabbed the stretcher's rail and phased the child with you, extending your dimensional shift to encompass another body for the first time in your life.

It's like carrying an anchor through water. The child's weight drags at your phase, threatens to collapse it, to strand you both inside the tunnel wall in a nightmare of merged molecules. You grit your teeth and push, pulling the child through the concrete, through three meters of solid infrastructure, and out the other side.
You collapse in a maintenance alcove, gasping, the child clutched against your chest. Your stabilizer is screaming, its amber light now flashing red. You've drained it. You've drained everything.
The child opens their eyes. They're silver. Pure, reflective silver, like mirrors set into a face that's too young for the fear it carries.

choices.text: "You're safe. I'm like you."
Check if Division 9 is pursuing

shd_021.title: Survival First
text: You press deeper into the shadows and let them pass. The child on the stretcher, the flickering outline of another Shadowkin, another experiment, another life being fed into the machine that Division 9 runs in the dark spaces between official policy and corporate greed. You let them carry that child away, and you tell yourself it's the smart play.

It's not your problem. You can barely keep yourself alive, barely keep your own phase under control, barely scrape enough credits together to keep the stabilizer charged. You're not a hero. You're not a rescuer. You're a twenty-year-old who sleeps in maintenance tunnels and eats cardboard nutrient bars and counts the hours until your only piece of functional technology dies.
The footsteps fade. The scanner's hum diminishes. The tunnel returns to its usual soundtrack of dripping water and distant machinery.
You sit in the dark for a long time, hating yourself with a thoroughness that borders on craftsmanship.

When you finally move, it's toward the surface. You need air. You need to not be underground anymore, surrounded by walls that remind you of all the walls you've passed through, all the barriers you've crossed, except the ones that matter.

choices.text: Head topside
Check your supplies first

shd_022.title: Deeper
text: The parallel corridor is older than the main tunnels, pre-dating the current city infrastructure by decades. The walls are bare concrete, unpainted, stained with mineral deposits that create abstract patterns in the lamplight. This section was sealed off during the last infrastructure overhaul, which means you're the only living thing down here.
Except you're not.

There's a mark on the wall. A symbol drawn in luminescent paint that glows faintly blue in the darkness. It's a circle bisected by a vertical line, with two smaller circles at the intersection points. You've seen this symbol before. Scratched into walls, painted on tunnel supports, etched into the concrete with something sharp. Always in the deep places. Always where the tunnels get old enough to remember.
Someone has been marking a path. And whoever they are, they know how to get to places that are supposed to be inaccessible. Which means they either have a key or they can walk through walls.
Which means they might be like you.

choices.text: Follow the symbols deeper
This could be a trap. Head back.

shd_023.title: The Fold
text: The woman's smile doesn't waver. 'We're called the Fold. And before you ask, no, it's not a cult. It's a network. Shadowkin looking out for Shadowkin, because nobody else in this city is going to do it for us.'

She gestures at the boy behind her. 'This is Ren. He's fourteen. He phased for the first time six months ago, fell through his apartment floor and ended up in the sewer system. His family reported him dead. Most do, when it starts. Easier to grieve a dead child than to explain a child who isn't entirely real.'

The big man steps forward. He's not Shadowkin, you can tell. He's solid in a way that your kind never quite manage, all dense muscle and gravity-obeying mass. 'I'm Thorne,' he says. His voice is deep enough to rattle pipes. 'I provide security. Not Shadowkin. Just someone who thinks kids shouldn't be hunted.'

The woman extends her hand. 'My name is Lira. I was the first. At least, the first one who lived long enough to learn control. I can teach you, if you'll let me. Things about the phase that you haven't figured out on your own. Things that will keep you alive.'

choices.text: Take her hand
"How do I know this isn't a Division 9 trap?"

shd_024.title: Recognition
text: The boy flinches when you speak. His outline ripples, the edges of his body blurring like heat haze, and for a second you can see through him to the tunnel wall behind. He's scared. You recognize the fear because you've worn it yourself for thirteen years.

'It's okay,' you say, and your voice comes out softer than you intended. You hold up your hand and let the phase flicker across your fingers, letting him see the way your skin becomes translucent, the way the light passes through you. Showing him that you understand. That you are the same impossible thing.
His eyes go wide. Then his face crumbles. Not into fear but into relief so intense it looks like grief. He starts crying, silent, shaking sobs that make his whole body phase in and out of visibility.
'He hasn't met another one,' the woman behind him says quietly. 'None of them believe they're not alone until they see it.'
You know exactly what she means. Because until thirty seconds ago, you didn't believe it either.

choices.text: Ask the woman who she is
Comfort the boy

shd_025.title: Guilt
text: Eight years and the guilt hasn't faded. It hasn't even dulled. It sits in your chest like a shard of glass that you can't remove because it's lodged too close to something vital. Every morning you wake up and the first thing you feel, before hunger, before fear, before the bone-deep exhaustion of being a thing that doesn't fully exist, is the weight of having left her behind.

She told you to run. She screamed it. It was her choice, her sacrifice, her love distilled into a single desperate command. But you were twelve years old and you had the power to walk through walls, to become untouchable, to reach through solid matter and pull someone to safety, and instead you ran.

You've looked for her. For years, you've looked. Hacked missing persons databases from public terminals. Bribed information brokers with credits you couldn't spare. Followed rumors through the Lower District's grapevine, whispers of a woman being held in a Division 9 facility, a woman who gave birth to a ghost.
Nothing. She's gone. Erased. The way they erase everything that threatens the official narrative that phase events are anomalies, not people.

choices.text: Focus on surviving. It's what she wanted.
Someday you'll find her. Or find the truth.

shd_026.title: Phasing the Yards
text: You find a gap between two stacks of crushed vehicle chassis and reach for the phase. The stabilizer protests, its amber light flickering as you override its dampening field. You can feel the device fighting you, trying to keep you solid, and for a moment you're caught between states, half-real, your left arm translucent while your right stays flesh-solid. It hurts. The partial phase always hurts, like being pulled in two directions by forces that don't agree on whether you exist.

You push through. Full phase. The world goes grey and distant, the sounds of the scrap yard muffled to whispers. You walk through the first stack like it's fog, stepping over the dense signatures of metal and ceramic, navigating by the faint glow of electrical components that burn brighter in phase-sight.

The electronics heap is thirty meters ahead. You can see it shining in the between-space, a constellation of residual charge, dead batteries and dormant circuits casting their own spectral light. You move toward it, each step costing you willpower, the hungry dark pressing closer with every second you spend outside of solid reality.

You find the power cells wedged between gutted datapads and corroded circuit boards. Three cells, compatible with your stabilizer. You grab them, feeling their weight anchor you, and snap back to reality with a gasp.

choices.text: Get out before anyone notices
Search for anything else useful

shd_027.title: Negotiation
text: You step out from between the stacks with your hands visible. The two scrappers spin toward you, pipe wrenches rising. The bigger one, a heavyset woman with a shaved head and burn scars across her forearms, takes a step forward.

'Wrong yard, shadow-freak,' she says. She's seen your kind before. The way you move, the slight blur at the edges, the way light doesn't quite land on you the same as it does on solid people. In the Lower District, Shadowkin are known. Not understood. Known.
'I need power cells,' you say. 'Three, compatible with a Kessler-type stabilizer. I can trade.'
'Trade what?' The second scrapper, a wiry man with cybernetic eyes that whirr when they refocus. 'You tunnel rats don't have anything worth trading.'

You do, though. You have the one thing that everyone in the scrap yards needs and nobody can buy: you can reach things that are buried too deep to dig out. You can phase through the stacks and retrieve components from the middle of compressed metal cubes that would take hours to cut open.

choices.text: Offer to retrieve buried components for them
This isn't going well. Phase out.

shd_028.title: Patience
text: You wait. Patience is a survival skill you've honed to a razor's edge. You crouch between the stacks, barely breathing, watching the patrol make their rounds. They move in a predictable pattern, sweeping the perimeter of the electronics section every twelve minutes. That gives you a window.

When they round the far corner, you move. Low, fast, using the shadows between the stacks as cover. Your feet are silent on the rain-slick concrete. The electronics heap rises ahead of you like a technological cairn, a monument to obsolescence piled three meters high.

You dig through the heap with practiced hands, pulling aside gutted monitors and cracked casings. The power cells are buried near the bottom, the good ones always are, the scrappers haven't dug deep enough to find them yet. Your fingers close around a cylindrical shape, smooth and cool, with the telltale weight of a charged cell.
Two cells. Not three, but two. It's enough for maybe thirty hours of stabilizer time. Not great, but better than nothing.
Then a flashlight beam sweeps across the heap. Someone's seen you.

choices.text: Run
Phase and disappear
Freeze and hope they pass

shd_029.title: Mira
text: The door opens six inches, held by a security chain. One eye peers out, dark brown, underlined by the kind of fatigue that caffeine can't touch. Then recognition. The chain rattles, the door swings wide, and Mira pulls you inside with a grip that's surprisingly strong for someone who weighs maybe fifty kilos soaking wet.

Her shop is a controlled explosion of technology. Workbenches covered in half-assembled devices, walls hung with tools and spare parts, a soldering station still smoking from recent use. Screens cover every surface, displaying code, schematics, news feeds, and what looks like a real-time map of phase events across the Lower District.

'Your stabilizer's dying,' Mira says, not a question. She can hear it, the frequency shift in the device's hum that indicates low charge. She built it, after all. She knows its voice the way a parent knows their child's cry.

Mira is not Shadowkin. She's baseline human, a former Meridian technician who left the corporation after she saw what Division 9 was doing with the phase-event subjects. She doesn't talk about what she saw. She doesn't need to. The nightmares that wake her screaming at three in the morning say enough.
'Sit down,' she says, already reaching for her toolkit. 'Let me look at it.'

choices.text: Let her work on the stabilizer
"Something's wrong, Mira. More than the battery."

shd_030.title: Paranoia
text: You turn and scan the crowd behind you. Old habit. The kind of habit that keeps you alive in a city where the wrong people are always looking for you. The market crowd flows like a river of faces, each one a potential threat, a potential witness, a potential pair of eyes connected to a comm unit connected to Division 9's informant network.

Nothing obvious. No grey coats. No scanner equipment. No one standing still in a moving crowd, which is the tell, the thing that Division 9 operatives can never quite get right. They watch too intently. They stand too still. They forget that in the Lower District, stillness is a luxury nobody can afford.

But then you see it. A drone. Small, no bigger than your fist, hovering at the edge of the noodle bar's awning. It's not a standard patrol model. Too small, too quiet, with a lens array that's focused directly on Mira's door.
Someone is watching the shop. And they've been watching long enough to set up surveillance.

choices.text: Warn Mira immediately
Try to disable the drone first

shd_031.title: Bao's Rumors
text: Bao ladles more broth into your bowl without being asked. 'Rumors,' she repeats, the word rolling around in her mouth like she's tasting it. 'Many rumors. Which kind you want? The kind that makes you worry or the kind that makes you run?'
'Both,' you say.

She leans on the counter, her weathered face close to yours. 'People disappearing from the tunnels. Not the usual kind of disappearing, not the overdoses and the cave-ins. Clean disappearing. People there one day, gone the next, no blood, no body, no trace. Three in the last week.'

She pauses, stirs the broth. 'And there's talk of a woman looking for people like you. Not Division people. Different. She has marks on her arms, like tattoos that move. She's been asking questions in the deep tunnels, leaving symbols on the walls. A circle with a line through it.'
Your spoon stops halfway to your mouth. You've seen those symbols. In the tunnels near your camp. On the walls of corridors that are supposed to be sealed.
'She's not hunting,' Bao says. 'She's recruiting. Big difference. But in this city, both can get you killed.'

choices.text: Go look for these symbols in the tunnels
Head to Mira's shop for the stabilizer

shd_032.title: Sabotage
text: You know a shortcut. A service duct that runs parallel to the main tunnel, barely wide enough for your shoulders. You phase through the wall to reach it, the between-space kissing your skin with its cold attention, and then you're running, half-solid, your feet passing through puddles instead of splashing through them.

The pumping station is ahead. You reach it two minutes before the Division 9 team, enough time to find the freight elevator's control panel and tear it open. The electronics inside are industrial-grade but old. You don't need to understand them. You just need to break them.

You phase your hand into the control board and solidify your fingers around the main relay. Then you pull. The relay comes out with a shower of sparks and the elevator groans, its motors seizing, the platform lurching to a stop between floors.

You hear the Division 9 team arrive. Hear them stop. Hear the scanner operator's curse as they find the elevator dead. Then the woman's voice, calm, professional: 'Alternate extraction. Route seven. Move.'
They have backup plans. Of course they do. But you've bought time. The question is what to do with it.

choices.text: Use the time to free the child
Get out and find help

shd_033.title: Intelligence
text: You follow them all the way to the pumping station, memorizing every turn, every junction, every access point. The route they take. The codes they punch into the security panels. The frequency their scanner operates on, which you can feel buzzing against your stabilizer like a wasp trapped under your collar.

Information is currency in the Lower District. The right information, given to the right people, can buy you safety, supplies, allies. And what you have now, the operational patterns of a Division 9 extraction team, is worth more than you've ever had before.

You watch them load the child into the freight elevator. The last thing you see before the doors close is the child's face, turned toward the tunnel, silver eyes open and staring, as if they can see you crouching in the shadows forty meters away.
Maybe they can. Shadowkin senses don't follow the normal rules.
The elevator ascends. The child is gone. But you have something now. You have knowledge. And knowledge, properly weaponized, is the only thing more dangerous than the ability to walk through walls.

choices.text: Find someone who can use this information
Head topside. You need to think.

shd_034.title: Connection
text: The child's silver eyes focus on your face. For a moment they're blank, glazed with the aftereffects of whatever Division 9 used to sedate them. Then clarity returns in a rush, and with it, fear.

'Don't,' you say quickly. 'Don't phase. I know you want to, I know your body is telling you to disappear, but if you phase here, in these tunnels, you could end up inside a wall. You could end up in the between. I need you to stay solid. Can you do that for me?'

The child nods. Small, trembling, but a nod. Their outline stabilizes, the flickering edges firming up. They're young, ten or eleven, with the angular features and dark skin that all Shadowkin share, the physical traits that mark you as products of the same dimensional accident.
'What's your name?' you ask.
'Pyx,' the child whispers. 'They said my name was Subject 47 but my name is Pyx.'
Subject 47. Forty-seven. Division 9 has catalogued at least forty-seven of your kind. The number sits in your stomach like a stone.

choices.text: "I'm going to get you somewhere safe, Pyx."
"Forty-seven? How many of us are there?"

shd_035.title: Pursuit
text: You can hear them. The Division 9 team, regrouping, their voices sharp with anger and professional urgency. Someone, the scanner operator, is tracking your phase signature. You can feel the scanner's pulse sweeping through the tunnels like sonar, each ping lighting up your position in whatever readout they're watching.
The child beside you is trembling. You need to move. Now.

You know these tunnels better than anyone who uses a map. You know the dead ends that aren't really dead ends if you can phase through the blocked sections. You know the water-filled sections where the scanner signal can't penetrate. You know the deep places, the old places, where the infrastructure is thick enough to block almost anything.
'Can you walk?' you ask the child.

They nod, sliding off the stretcher on unsteady legs. They're small and thin and drugged and terrified, and you're about to lead them through a maze of underground tunnels while being hunted by people with technology specifically designed to find your kind.
You've had better nights. But not many.

choices.text: Head for the water-filled sections to lose the scanner
Go deep, to the old tunnels below the reactor shielding

shd_036.title: The Deep Place
text: The symbols lead you down. Past the maintenance levels, past the water mains and the sewage processors, past the old transit lines that haven't carried passengers in twenty years. Down into the infrastructure's bones, where the concrete gives way to raw rock and the air tastes like rust and deep earth.
The symbols get brighter the deeper you go. Fresher paint, more carefully drawn. Whoever is marking this path, they've been here recently. Maybe they're here now.

You reach a junction where four tunnels converge into a natural cavern, a pocket in the bedrock that the city builders incorporated rather than filled. The space is large enough to hold a crowd. And it has.

There are bedrolls arranged in neat rows. A makeshift kitchen with a camp stove and stacked containers. A workbench covered in tools and phase stabilizer components. And on the far wall, painted in luminescent blue that fills the cavern with an unearthly glow, a mural. Figures emerging from shadow, their outlines blurred, their hands reaching for each other across a field of darkness.
This is a sanctuary. A place built by and for Shadowkin. And you've been living in the tunnels above it, alone and afraid, for years.

choices.text: Explore the sanctuary
Call out. See if anyone's here.

shd_037.title: Joining
text: You take Lira's hand. Her grip is firm and warm and solid in a way that surprises you. You expected another Shadowkin's touch to feel different, phase-shifted, unstable. But her hand is the most real thing you've touched in years.
'Good,' she says. 'Come with us. There are things you need to see.'

She leads you deeper into the tunnels, Thorne bringing up the rear with the quiet watchfulness of a professional bodyguard. Ren walks beside you, stealing glances, his fear slowly being replaced by something that looks like hope. You wonder if that's what you looked like when you saw his outline flicker. Probably.

Lira talks as she walks. The Fold started with her, five years ago, when she emerged from a phase that lasted three days and found herself in a part of the city's underground that no map recorded. She'd been alone for so long she'd forgotten what another voice sounded like. Then she found another Shadowkin. Then another. Then she started looking deliberately.

'There are twenty-three of us now,' Lira says. 'Twenty-four, with you. We look out for each other. We share what we know about the phase, about Division 9, about surviving in a world that thinks we're ghosts.'
Twenty-four. You're not alone. The realization hits you like a wall you didn't know was there.

choices.text: Follow Lira to the Fold's sanctuary
Ask about Division 9's activities

shd_038.title: Suspicion
text: Lira's smile fades. Not into anger. Into understanding. 'Fair question. The kind of question that keeps you alive. I respect that.'

She rolls up her sleeve. The tattoos on her arm aren't tattoos at all, you realize. They're phase scars, places where her body has been forced in and out of dimensional alignment so many times that the skin has changed, darkened, developed patterns that shift when she moves. Like your own skin does, in the places where the phasing is worst.

'I spent eighteen months in a Division 9 facility,' she says, her voice flat, emotionless, the way people sound when they're describing things that would break them if they let the feelings in. 'Ward C, Sublevel 4, Meridian Applied Sciences Building, North Sector. They catalogued me as Subject 12. They ran tests. They pushed my phase limits to see how far I could go before I stopped coming back. They found out.'

She holds up her left hand. It's translucent. Not phased, not flickering, just permanently see-through, like glass shaped into flesh. You can see the bones inside, the tendons, the blood vessels carrying blood that looks darker than it should.
'Part of me didn't come back,' she says. 'They pushed too hard and left a piece of me in the between. So no, I'm not Division 9. I'm the reason Division 9 changed their protocols.'

choices.text: "I believe you. I'll come with you."
"I'm sorry. What can you teach me?"

shd_039.title: Kindred
text: You kneel down to Ren's eye level. He's small for fourteen, stunted by malnutrition and the metabolic cost of uncontrolled phasing. His body burns energy at twice the normal rate, trying to maintain cohesion in a state that wasn't designed for permanence. You know because yours does the same thing.
'Hey,' you say. 'The phasing. It's scary. I know. I've been doing it since I was seven.'
His sobbing slows. 'Seven?' A whisper. 'That's... that's so long.'
'Yeah. And I'm still here. Still solid. Most of the time.' You hold out your hand, palm up. 'See? Solid. You're going to be okay. It gets easier. Not easy, but easier. The first year is the worst.'

Ren reaches out and touches your palm. His fingers phase through yours for a split second before solidifying, the contact establishing something deeper than physical touch. A recognition between two beings who exist in the same impossible space between real and not-real.
'I thought I was dying,' Ren says. 'When it started. I thought I was dissolving.'
'You're not dying,' you say. 'You're just becoming something that doesn't have a name yet.'

choices.text: Ask the woman where she's taking you
Travel with the group

shd_040.title: Escape from the Yards
text: You run. Through the gaps between the stacks, over piles of crushed metal that shift and groan under your feet, around corners that all look the same in the dark. Behind you, shouts. The scrappers have seen you, and they're not happy about losing inventory.

A wrench flies past your head and clangs off a stack of engine blocks. You duck, stumble, catch yourself on a jagged edge of metal that slices your palm. Blood, hot and real, proof that you're solid, proof that you're here. You can't phase right now, the stabilizer is in emergency mode, its red light blinking a warning that means don't you dare.

You vault over a low wall of compacted scrap and drop into an alley on the other side of the yard. The shouts fade behind you. The scrappers won't follow past their territory. They're possessive but not stupid.
You lean against a wall and catch your breath. The power cells are heavy in your pocket. Your hand is bleeding. Your stabilizer is screaming. But you got what you came for.

choices.text: Head to Mira's shop to install the cells
Go back to the tunnels and rest

shd_041.title: Scavenging
text: While you're here, you dig deeper. Under the power cells, buried in the sediment of technological waste, your fingers close around something unexpected. A data chip, military-grade, with the Meridian Applied Sciences logo embossed on its casing. It's undamaged, which shouldn't be possible if it was thrown away through normal channels.
Someone didn't throw this away. Someone hid it here.

You pocket the chip along with the power cells. The chip probably contains nothing useful, maybe old inventory data or personnel records. But military-grade encryption on a discarded chip hidden in a scrap yard suggests something worth protecting. Or something someone wanted to disappear.
A noise behind you. Boots on metal. Time to go.

choices.text: Get out fast
Phase through the stacks to escape unseen

shd_042.title: The Deal
text: The scrappers exchange a look. You can see the calculation happening behind their eyes. Disgust for your kind fighting against the practical value of what you're offering. Greed wins. It usually does.

'There's a compacted cube in section nine,' the woman says. 'Pre-war military hardware, crushed and sealed. Our cutters can't get through the alloy. You phase in, grab what's inside, we give you your cells and a hot meal. Deal?'

You nod. She leads you to the cube, a two-meter block of compressed metal so dense it looks like solid rock. You place your hands on the surface and feel for the contents, letting your phase-sense reach into the compressed layers. There's something inside. Something that hums with residual charge.

You phase your arms in up to the elbows and feel around. Your fingers close on a shape, cylindrical, warm, vibrating with contained energy. You pull it out and stagger back, holding a device you don't recognize. The scrappers' eyes go wide.

'Well,' the woman says softly. 'That's a pre-war phase emitter. Division 9 has been looking for those for years.' She looks at you. 'I'll give you the cells, the meal, and some advice: get rid of that thing before someone kills you for it.'

choices.text: Take the cells and keep the emitter
Take the cells and leave the emitter

shd_043.title: Ghost Exit
text: You phase. Full shift, damn the stabilizer, damn the battery drain. The world goes grey and the scrap yard becomes a forest of glowing outlines, the residual energy signatures of dead machines painting the between-space in ghostly light.

You walk through the stacks like they're curtains. Through walls of compressed metal, through locked gates, through the perimeter fence and out onto the street beyond. Behind you, the scrappers stare at the empty space where you were standing, their flashlight beams sweeping through air that no longer contains you.

You snap back to reality on the other side of the fence, gasping, your stabilizer whining at a pitch that makes your teeth vibrate. The battery indicator is deep red now. Maybe six hours left. Maybe less.
But you're out. And you have what you came for.

choices.text: Get to Mira's shop before the stabilizer dies
Head for the tunnels. Rest first.

shd_044.title: Caught
text: The flashlight finds you. Pins you against the electronics heap like a butterfly on a board. Two scrappers approach, one with a pipe wrench, the other with a spot welder that crackles with blue-white malice.
'Tunnel rat,' the welder says. 'Stealing from our heap.'
You hold up the power cells. 'I need these. I'll pay, or I'll work for them. Please.'

The wrench-carrier studies you. Her eyes narrow when she sees the stabilizer around your neck, the blinking red light, the telltale shimmer at the edges of your outline. 'You're one of those phasing freaks,' she says. Not cruel, exactly. Just stating a fact the way you'd state the weather.

'I am,' you say. No point denying it. 'And without these cells, I'm a phasing freak who can't control the phasing. Which means I might accidentally walk through your friend's ribcage. Neither of us wants that.'
A pause. Then the wrench-carrier laughs. 'Take the cells, shadow-freak. Take them and get gone. And don't come back to this section again.'

choices.text: Take the cells and leave
"Thank you. I won't forget this."

shd_045.title: Repairs
text: You sit on the stool next to Mira's workbench and lift the stabilizer over your head. The moment it leaves your neck, you feel the difference. The phase response stirring in your chest, the tingling at your fingertips, the subtle loosening of your connection to solid reality. Without the stabilizer, you're maybe thirty minutes from an involuntary phase. Maybe less, if something startles you.

Mira works with the focused intensity of someone defusing a bomb. Her hands are steady, her tools precise. She opens the stabilizer's casing and examines the internals with a magnifying lens, making small sounds of displeasure.
'The frequency modulator is degrading,' she says. 'It's been running at full output too long. The dampening field isn't calibrated for extended use. I built this to be a stopgap, not a lifestyle.'
'I don't have a lot of options, Mira.'

'I know. Give me twenty minutes. I can recalibrate the modulator and swap the power cell. It'll buy you another month, maybe two. But after that, you need a real solution. Corporate-grade. The kind that Division 9 uses on their test subjects.'
She doesn't look up as she says this, but you can hear the implication. The only people with that technology are the people who want to cage you.

choices.text: "Where would I even get corporate-grade tech?"
"What did you see at Division 9, Mira?"

shd_046.title: Something Wrong
text: Mira stops reaching for her toolkit. She looks at you, really looks, with the clinical attention she usually reserves for malfunctioning hardware. 'Tell me,' she says.

'The phases are getting longer,' you say. 'And deeper. Last week I phased in my sleep and woke up inside the wall. Not halfway through it. Inside it. I could feel the concrete around me, the rebar passing through my ribcage. It took me four minutes to phase back out. Four minutes of being embedded in solid matter.'
Mira's face does something complicated. You've seen the expression before, on your mother's face, the war between clinical assessment and emotional investment. 'How often?' she asks.

'Every other night. Sometimes more. And the between-space is getting... louder. When I phase, I can hear something there. Like a signal. Like something trying to communicate through the dark between dimensions.'

Mira sits down slowly. 'The other Shadowkin I've examined, three of them, have reported the same thing. The phases intensifying. The between-space becoming active. Something is changing in the dimensional barrier, and your bodies are responding to it.'
She meets your eyes. 'I think whatever caused the original rift, the experiment that created your kind, isn't over. I think it's accelerating.'

choices.text: "What happens if it keeps accelerating?"
"Other Shadowkin? You've met others?"

shd_047.title: Warning
text: You bang on the door. Hard. Not the code knock, the emergency knock. Mira opens the door in three seconds flat, weapon in hand, a compact taser that looks homemade and dangerous.
'Drone,' you say. 'On the noodle bar's awning. Surveillance lens, pointed right at your door.'

Mira doesn't hesitate. She pulls you inside, locks the door, and moves to a bank of screens on her workbench. Her fingers fly across a keyboard, pulling up her own surveillance feeds, counter-surveillance protocols activating with the practiced efficiency of someone who's always expected this day to come.

'I see it,' she says. 'Meridian model, but the firmware's been modified. It's not corporate standard. Someone rented this hardware, which means private contract, which means Division 9 is outsourcing again.'
She turns to face you. 'They've found me. Or they've found you, and followed you here. Either way, we need to move. Both of us. Now.'
She grabs a bag from under her workbench, pre-packed, the bug-out bag that everyone in the Lower District keeps ready for the day their luck runs out.

choices.text: "I know a safe place. The tunnels."
"We should split up. They can't follow both of us."

shd_048.title: Countermeasures
text: The drone is small but not invulnerable. You've learned a few things about technology from Mira, enough to know that a drone this size runs on a single motor with a limited battery. Disrupt the motor and it drops. The question is how to reach it without being seen.

You edge along the wall of the noodle bar, staying below the drone's camera angle. The awning provides cover. You can see the drone's underbelly now, the tiny propeller housing, the lens array with its red recording light.

You reach up with one hand and let the phase ripple through just your fingers. The fingertips go translucent, passing through the drone's casing, and you feel the motor inside. A tiny electromagnetic coil spinning at high speed. You solidify your fingers around it.
The motor stops. The drone drops into your hand, dead, its lens dark. You pocket it quickly, a piece of evidence, a piece of proof that someone is watching.
Then you knock on Mira's door.

choices.text: Show Mira the drone
Enter and explain what you found

shd_049.title: Finding Allies
text: You need help. You need people who understand what Division 9 is doing and have the resources to fight back. The problem is that in the Lower District, trust is the most expensive commodity, and you're broke in every currency that matters.
There's Mira. She has the technical knowledge, the inside understanding of Division 9's operations. But she's one person with a soldering iron, not an army.

There are the tunnel communities. The underground dwellers who've carved out lives in the maintenance network, people who value privacy and hate corporate intrusion with a passion born from experience. They might shelter you. They might turn you in. Down here, it depends on the day.

And then there's the rumor. The one Bao mentioned, or that you've heard in whispers passed through the tunnel network. A woman with moving tattoos, recruiting Shadowkin. The Fold. If they're real, they might be exactly what you need. If they're a Division 9 honeypot, walking into their sanctuary would be the last mistake you ever make.

choices.text: Go to Mira
Search for the Fold in the deep tunnels
Try the tunnel communities

shd_050.title: Promise
text: Pyx looks at you with those silver eyes, and you see yourself at seven. Falling through floors. Phasing through walls. Losing the only person who loved you to people who saw you as data to be collected.
'Where is safe?' Pyx asks. It's a real question. Not rhetorical. The child genuinely wants to know if such a place exists, and the honesty of the question hits you like a fist to the sternum.

You don't know. You've never been safe. You've been alive, which isn't the same thing. But you know the tunnels, and you know how to disappear, and right now, disappearing is the best gift you can give this child.

'I know a place,' you say. You're thinking of the deep tunnels, the sections near the reactor shielding where scanner signals can't penetrate. It's not safe. Nowhere is safe. But it's hidden, and hidden is the closest thing to safe that either of you is ever going to get.
You take Pyx's hand. It's small and cold and phases in and out of solidity with a rhythm that matches their frightened heartbeat. You hold on tight.

choices.text: Head for the deep tunnels
Try to reach Mira's shop topside

shd_051.title: The Number
text: Pyx's face darkens. 'They had a list. On the wall of the room where they kept me. Names, numbers, status. Subject 1 through Subject 47. Some had red lines through them. Red means terminated. More than half had red lines.'
Your blood runs cold. Not chilled. Frozen. Solid in your veins in a way that has nothing to do with phasing.
'They terminate subjects?' you ask, and your voice is remarkably steady for someone whose world is collapsing.

'When the experiments fail. When the subject can't be controlled. When they learn what they need and the subject isn't useful anymore.' Pyx recites it without emotion, the way children recite things they've heard adults say so many times the words have lost their meaning. 'Subject 12 was the oldest one. She escaped before I got there. They were very angry about Subject 12.'

Subject 12. Lira. If the woman in the tunnels, the one with the moving tattoos and the transparent hand, is Subject 12, then the Fold isn't just a support group. It's a resistance built by someone who survived Division 9's worst and came back for the others.
All the more reason to find her.

choices.text: Get Pyx to safety first
Ask Pyx what else they remember

shd_052.title: The Water Tunnels
text: The water-filled sections are the worst part of the tunnel network. Knee-deep in places, chest-deep in others, the water black and cold and thick with decades of chemical runoff. It stinks of iron and ammonia and something organic that you don't want to identify. But the water blocks scanner signals the way lead blocks radiation, and right now, invisibility is worth any amount of filth.

You wade through the muck, one hand on the wall to steady yourself, the other gripping Pyx's hand. The child shivers but doesn't complain. Division 9 has already taught them that there are worse things than cold water and darkness.

The tunnel opens into a wider channel where the water is only ankle-deep. Here, in the quietest part of the city's forgotten infrastructure, you stop. You listen. Nothing. No footsteps. No scanner hum. No pursuit.
You've lost them. For now.

Pyx sinks down against the wall, exhausted. You stand guard, listening to the drip of water and the distant groan of pipes, and you think about what comes next. You have a child who needs protection, enemies who won't stop looking, and a stabilizer that's running on borrowed time.

choices.text: Rest here. Plan tomorrow.
Keep moving toward the deep sanctuary

shd_053.title: The Sanctuary
text: The sanctuary is larger than you first realized. Beyond the main cavern, side passages lead to sleeping quarters, a medical bay improvised from salvaged equipment, and a training area where the marks on the walls suggest regular practice. Phase practice. The scorch marks and displaced concrete tell a story of people learning to control something that was never meant to be controlled.

Shadowkin move through the space with a quiet purpose that reminds you of ants in a colony. Each one has the telltale signs: the shifting outline, the occasional flicker of transparency, the way light bends slightly wrong around their bodies. But they're stable. More stable than you. Their movements are confident, their phase effects controlled, deliberate.
A woman approaches you. Lira, if you haven't met her yet, or a familiar face if you have. She's flanked by others, their expressions a mix of curiosity and caution.

'Welcome to the Fold,' she says. 'You've been surviving alone for a long time. That ends now. But first, there are things you need to understand about what you are, what we all are, and what's coming.'

choices.text: "Tell me everything."
"What's coming?"

shd_054.title: Hello Darkness
text: 'Hello?' Your voice echoes off the cavern walls, bouncing between the stalactites of concrete and rebar that hang from the ceiling. The echo comes back changed, layered with harmonics that shouldn't be there, as if the cavern itself is answering.

Then shadows move. Not the normal way shadows move when a light source shifts. These shadows peel away from the walls, take shape, become figures. Three of them, emerging from the darkness with the liquid grace of people who've learned to wear shadows the way others wear clothing.
Shadowkin. All of them. And they've been watching you since you entered the cavern.

'Took you long enough,' one of them says. A young man, early twenties, with phase scars running up his neck and across his shaved scalp. 'We've been leaving symbols for you for three months. Started to think you couldn't read.'

'He was being careful,' another voice says. A woman emerges from a side passage, and you recognize the moving tattoos, the glass-clear left hand. Lira. 'Careful is good. Careful keeps you alive. But careful also keeps you alone, and alone is something we can't afford to be anymore.'

choices.text: "You've been looking for me specifically?"
"Why can't we afford to be alone?"

shd_055.title: Division 9 Intel
text: Lira's expression hardens. 'Division 9 has been escalating. More teams, more resources, more aggressive tactics. They used to observe and document. Now they're capturing. Extracting. Three Shadowkin have been taken in the last month alone. The youngest was eight.'

She gestures at the tunnel walls as she walks. 'They're running a program. Project Threshold. The official goal is to understand the dimensional phase phenomenon. The real goal is to weaponize it. Imagine soldiers who can walk through walls, who can't be caged, who can pass through enemy defenses like ghosts. That's what Meridian wants.'
'And the ones who can't be turned into weapons?' you ask.

'Dissected. Studied at the cellular level. Disposed of.' Lira's glass hand catches the lamplight, and inside it you can see her pulse, slow and steady, the heartbeat of someone who's already survived the worst thing imaginable. 'They pushed me until my hand stayed in the between. Then they tried to push harder. I escaped before they could take the rest of me.'
'That's why the Fold exists. Not just to hide. To fight back. To protect the ones they haven't found yet. To dismantle Project Threshold before it produces results.'

choices.text: "I want to help. I want to fight."
"How do you fight an entire corporation?"

shd_056.title: Training Offer
text: Lira leads you through the tunnels as she talks. 'The phase isn't random. It follows rules, just not the rules you're used to. Your body exists in a state of dimensional superposition, partially here, partially in the between-space. The stabilizer keeps you anchored to this side, but it's a crutch, not a cure.'

'I can teach you to anchor yourself without the stabilizer. To phase deliberately, with precision, entering and exiting solid matter at exact points. To extend your phase to objects, to other people. To see in the between-space, to navigate it, to use it as a shortcut through physical reality.'

She stops and turns to face you, her glass hand raised. 'And I can teach you the most important thing of all: how to come back. Every Shadowkin who's been lost, every one who phased and never returned, they lost their anchor. They forgot what it feels like to be solid. I can teach you to remember.'
Her eyes are intense, burning with the conviction of someone who's built their purpose from their pain. 'Will you let me?'

choices.text: "Yes. Teach me."
"First show me the sanctuary."

shd_057.title: Return Underground
text: You descend through the maintenance hatch, the familiar embrace of darkness and concrete closing around you. The tunnels are quiet tonight. The usual ambient sounds, dripping water, distant machinery, the scurry of rats, feel muted. Like the underground is holding its breath.

Your camp is undisturbed. The bedroll, the lamp, the duffel bag with its meager contents. Everything exactly as you left it. You sit down and install the new power cells in the stabilizer, feeling the device hum back to life with renewed energy. The amber light steadies. Full charge. Maybe forty hours.

Forty hours of being solid. Forty hours of control. Then you're back to scavenging, back to trading, back to the endless cycle of maintaining the one piece of technology that keeps you from dissolving into the space between spaces.

There has to be something better. Something more than this slow-motion survival, this existence measured in battery charge and nutrient bars and the distance between you and the nearest Division 9 patrol.

choices.text: Sleep. You need rest.
You can't sleep. Go looking for answers.

shd_058.title: The Emitter
text: You pocket the phase emitter. It vibrates against your hip, a gentle thrum that resonates with something deep inside you, the part of you that exists between dimensions. It feels like recognition. Like a tool designed for a hand that hasn't existed until now.
The scrapper watches you leave with an expression that mixes relief and concern. 'Your funeral,' she calls after you. 'When Division 9 comes knocking, I never saw you.'

On the street outside the yards, you examine the emitter more closely. It's old, pre-war manufacture, a time when the technology that created Shadowkin was still being actively developed. Before the accident. Before the rift. Before the experiment that tore a hole in the dimensional barrier and created a generation of children who couldn't fully exist in one reality.
The emitter could be the key to understanding what you are. Or it could be a beacon that leads every Division 9 scanner in the district straight to your location.
Right now, it's both. And you need to figure out which one matters more.

choices.text: Take it to Mira for analysis
Hide it in the tunnels and deal with it later

shd_059.title: Options
text: Mira snaps the stabilizer casing closed and hands it back to you. 'There are three options, none of them good. One: steal it from a Division 9 facility. Suicidal, but comprehensive. Two: find a pre-war cache. The original research labs had commercial-grade stabilizers before the project went classified. Some of that hardware might still exist in decommissioned sites. Three: make one from scratch, which requires components I can't source in the Lower District.'

She leans back, arms crossed. 'Option two is your best bet. There's a rumor, old, probably unreliable, about a research annex beneath the Foundry. The original lab where the dimensional experiments were conducted. If any of the old equipment survived, that's where it would be.'

The Foundry. The industrial heart of the Lower District, a maze of factories and processing plants that never stop running. Going underground there means navigating active industrial infrastructure, heat exchangers, pressure vessels, automated systems that don't care if you're in the way.
'If I were you,' Mira says, 'I wouldn't go alone.'

choices.text: "I don't have anyone to go with."
"The Foundry. I'll find a way."

shd_060.title: Mira's Past
text: Mira's hands stop moving. She doesn't look at you. She looks at the stabilizer in her hands as if it contains the answer to a question she's been asking for years.

'I was a biomedical engineer,' she says. 'Level 3 clearance, Meridian Applied Sciences, Division 9. I thought I was helping. The project brief said we were studying dimensional anomalies to prevent rift events. Protecting the city. Saving lives.'

She sets down the soldering iron. 'Then they brought in the first subject. A girl. Eleven years old. She could walk through walls the way you and I walk through doors. They put her in a containment cell made of phase-dampening alloy and they ran tests. Stress tests. How far can she phase? How long? What happens if we force her to phase continuously for six hours? For twelve? For twenty-four?'

Her voice is flat. Dead. 'She stopped being able to solidify on day three. They kept testing for two more weeks. When it was over, they had their data. She had nothing. She was nothing. A shadow without a body, a consciousness trapped in the between-space, screaming on a frequency that only their instruments could hear.'
'I resigned the next day. They tried to stop me. I have the scars under my sleeve to prove it. But I got out. And I've been trying to undo what I helped build ever since.'

choices.text: "You're helping. The stabilizer has kept me alive."
"That girl. Is she still... there? In the between?"

shd_061.title: Acceleration
text: Mira pulls up a graph on one of her screens. A curve that starts flat and then rises exponentially, climbing toward a peak that the graph can't contain. 'This is the phase frequency data I've collected from every Shadowkin I've examined over the past three years. The dimensional barrier is weakening. The phases are getting stronger, lasting longer, pulling your kind deeper into the between-space each time.'
'At current rates, within six months, no stabilizer will be strong enough to keep you anchored. Within a year, the barrier might thin enough for things to cross in the other direction.'
'Things?' you ask.

'The between-space isn't empty,' Mira says. 'You know that. You've felt it. The attention. The hunger. Something lives there, or exists there, or occupies that space in a way that defies our definitions of living and existing. And the barrier that keeps it there is the same barrier that your kind passes through every time you phase.'
'Every phase weakens the barrier a little more. And something on the other side is pushing.'

choices.text: "Then we need to stop phasing. All of us."
"Or we need to strengthen the barrier."

shd_062.title: Others Like You
text: 'Three,' Mira says. 'Two men and a woman. They found me the same way you did, through the underground network, through whispers and referrals. They came for stabilizer repairs and stayed long enough for me to run diagnostics.'

'They're out there, living in the margins, just like you. Hiding in tunnels, sleeping in shifts, keeping their stabilizers charged and their heads down. And every one of them described the same thing: the phases getting worse, the between-space getting louder, the feeling that something is changing.'

She hesitates, then: 'There's a group. They call themselves the Fold. A Shadowkin collective, operating out of the deep tunnels. Their leader is a woman named Lira, Subject 12, the one who escaped Division 9. She's been gathering your kind, building something. A network. A resistance. I don't know the details, but I know they're planning something.'
'Lira came to me once. Asked for stabilizer blueprints. Enough to outfit a small army. I gave them to her. Whatever she's building, she's building it to last.'

choices.text: "How do I find the Fold?"
"What are they planning?"

shd_063.title: Escape with Mira
text: You lead Mira through the maintenance hatch behind her building, down into the tunnel network. She follows without hesitation, her bug-out bag on her back, her taser in her hand. She's not a tunnel dweller, not built for darkness and confined spaces, but she moves with the grim determination of someone who's been preparing for this day since she walked out of Division 9's facility.

Behind you, above you, you can hear the sounds of a raid beginning. The crash of her shop door being breached. The stamp of boots. The bark of voices giving orders. Division 9 is thorough, and they're angry, and they've just found an empty shop instead of the targets they were expecting.
'They'll follow us down,' Mira says, not panicking, just stating facts. 'They have tunnel maps. Probably better ones than yours.'

'They don't have my maps,' you say. Because your maps aren't maps. They're routes that pass through walls, shortcuts that only exist for someone who can step through solid matter. You know paths that no map could chart, because no mapmaker could walk them.
You take Mira's hand. 'Hold on. I'm going to phase us through a wall.'
'Through a-' she starts, then the world goes sideways and the wall becomes mist and you pull her through into a passage that exists only in the spaces between.

choices.text: Head for the Fold's sanctuary
Find a secure hiding spot first

shd_064.title: Separation
text: Mira nods. She understands the logic, even if she doesn't like it. 'You're the primary target. If they're tracking your phase signature, splitting up means I can get clear while they follow you.'
'I can lose them in the tunnels,' you say. 'I know paths they can't follow.'

'I know.' She pulls something from her workbench, a data chip, and presses it into your hand. 'Everything I have on Division 9. Research data, facility locations, personnel files. If something happens to me, this information needs to survive.'
She meets your eyes. 'Find the Fold. Find Lira. Give her this. And tell her the phase acceleration is real. She needs to know.'

You want to argue, want to tell her that nothing's going to happen to her, but the sounds from outside are getting closer. Running out of time. You grip the chip, grip her shoulder, and then you're moving, phasing through the back wall, leaving Mira to make her own escape through the network of safe houses she's maintained for exactly this contingency.
The last thing you see before the wall closes around you is her face, lit by the glow of her screens, calm and certain and already planning her next move.

choices.text: Get underground. Find the Fold.
Check if Mira gets out safely first

shd_065.title: Evidence
text: Mira takes the drone from you and immediately opens it on her workbench, her fingers working with the practiced speed of someone who's disassembled a thousand devices. Inside, she finds the transmitter, the recording module, the control chip.

'Modified Meridian hardware,' she confirms. 'But the control chip is aftermarket. Black market. Someone bought a corporate drone and reprogrammed it for private surveillance.' She pries out the recording module and plugs it into her system. 'Let's see what it's been watching.'

The screen fills with footage. Hours of it. Mira's door, the street outside, the faces of everyone who's entered and left. And there, timestamped three days ago, a clear image of you approaching the shop. Your face. Your stabilizer. Your outline, blurred at the edges in the way that marks you as Shadowkin.
'They know what you are,' Mira says quietly. 'And they know you come here. We're both compromised.'
She pulls the chip and crushes it under her heel. Then she reaches for her bug-out bag.

choices.text: "We need to leave. Now."
"We should split up to be safe."

shd_066.title: Tunnel Communities
text: The tunnel communities exist in the spaces between official infrastructure. Clusters of people who've fallen through the cracks of the city above, building lives from scrap and stubbornness in the maintenance network's unused sections. They're not organized, not led, just connected by proximity and mutual survival.

You approach the nearest community, a cluster of shelters built around a junction where warm air vents from the industrial sector above. Maybe thirty people, living in lean-tos made of salvaged panels and thermal blankets. A few nod as you pass. They know you. The shadow-kid from the east tunnels. Not one of them, exactly, but tolerated. A known quantity.
